title: National Park Service

infobox.value: * [Main Interior Building](/wiki/Main_Interior_Building "Main Interior Building") * 1849 C Street NW * [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") * 20240, U.S.
* [Jessica Bowron](/wiki/Jessica_Bowron "Jessica Bowron"), Acting [Director of the National Park Service](/wiki/Director_of_the_National_Park_Service "Director of the National Park Service")
[United States Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior")
* [National Park Service Organic Act](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Organic_Act "National Park Service Organic Act")

(**NPS**) is an [agency](/wiki/List_of_federal_agencies_in_the_United_States "List of federal agencies in the United States") of the [United States federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"), within the [United States Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior"). The service manages all [national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_the_United_States "List of national parks of the United States"); most [national monuments](/wiki/National_monument_(United_States) "National monument (United States)"); and other natural, historical, and recreational properties, with various title designations.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4) The [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") created the agency on August 25, 1916, through the [National Park Service Organic Act](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Organic_Act "National Park Service Organic Act").[[5]](#cite_note-national-5) Its headquarters is in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), within the main headquarters of the Department of the Interior.

The **National Park Service** (**NPS**) is an [agency](/wiki/List_of_federal_agencies_in_the_United_States "List of federal agencies in the United States") of the [United States federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"), within the [United States Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior"). The service manages all [national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_the_United_States "List of national parks of the United States"); most [national monuments](/wiki/National_monument_(United_States) "National monument (United States)"); and other natural, historical, and recreational properties, with various title designations.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4) The [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") created the agency on August 25, 1916, through the [National Park Service Organic Act](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Organic_Act "National Park Service Organic Act").[[5]](#cite_note-national-5) Its headquarters is in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), within the main headquarters of the Department of the Interior.

The NPS employs about 20,000 people in 433 units covering over 85 million acres (0.34 million km2) in [all 50 states](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States "List of states and territories of the United States"), the District of Columbia, and [U.S. territories](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States").[[1]](#cite_note-Emp1-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4) In 2019, the service had more than 279,000 volunteers.[[1]](#cite_note-Emp1-1) The agency is charged with preserving the ecological and historical integrity of the places entrusted to its management and with making them available for public use and enjoyment.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the National Park Service](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Park_Service "History of the National Park Service")

See also: [National Park Service Organic Act](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Organic_Act "National Park Service Organic Act")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Natlparks_and_RRs_1916.jpg/250px-Natlparks_and_RRs_1916.jpg)](/wiki/File:Natlparks_and_RRs_1916.jpg)

In 1916, a portfolio of nine major parks was published to generate interest. Printed on each brochure was a map showing the parks and principal railroad connections.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/USPS_National_Park_Service_1934.jpg/250px-USPS_National_Park_Service_1934.jpg)](/wiki/File:USPS_National_Park_Service_1934.jpg)

In 1934, a series of ten postage stamps was issued to commemorate the reorganization and expansion of the National Park Service.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/NPS_Preliminary_Survey_Personnel_GSNP.jpg/250px-NPS_Preliminary_Survey_Personnel_GSNP.jpg)](/wiki/File:NPS_Preliminary_Survey_Personnel_GSNP.jpg)

NPS Preliminary Survey party, [Great Smoky Mountains](/wiki/Great_Smoky_Mountains "Great Smoky Mountains"), 1931

Artist [George Catlin](/wiki/George_Catlin "George Catlin"), during an 1832 trip to the Dakotas, was perhaps the first to suggest the concept of a national park. Indian civilization, wildlife, and wilderness were all in danger, wrote Catlin, unless they could be preserved "by some great protecting policy of government ... in a magnificent park ... A nation's Park, containing man and beast, in all the wild[ness] and freshness of their nature's beauty!"[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") was created as the first [national park](/wiki/National_park "National park") in the United States.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) In 1872, there was no [state government](/wiki/State_governments_of_the_United_States "State governments of the United States") to manage it (Wyoming [was a U.S. territory](/wiki/History_of_Wyoming "History of Wyoming") at that time), so the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") managed it directly through the army, including the famed African American [Buffalo Soldier](/wiki/Buffalo_Soldier "Buffalo Soldier") units.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The movement for an independent agency to oversee these federal lands was spearheaded by [business magnate](/wiki/Business_magnate "Business magnate") and [conservationist](/wiki/Conservation_movement "Conservation movement") [Stephen Mather](/wiki/Stephen_Mather "Stephen Mather").[[10]](#cite_note-10) With the help of journalist [Robert Sterling Yard](/wiki/Robert_Sterling_Yard "Robert Sterling Yard"), Mather ran a publicity campaign for the [Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior"). They wrote numerous articles that praised the scenic and historic qualities of the parks and their possibilities for educational, inspirational, and recreational benefits.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

This campaign resulted in the creation of the NPS. On August 25, 1916, President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") signed the [National Park Service Organic Act](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Organic_Act "National Park Service Organic Act") that mandated the agency "to conserve the scenery and the natural and historic objects and wildlife therein, and to provide for the enjoyment of the same in such manner and by such means as will leave them unimpaired for the enjoyment of future generations".[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Mather became the first director of the newly formed NPS.[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14)

On March 3, 1933, President [Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover "Herbert Hoover") signed the Reorganization Act of 1933. The act gave the president the authority to transfer national monuments from one governmental department to another.[[15]](#cite_note-:5-15) Later that summer, new president [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") made use of this power after NPS Deputy Director [Horace M. Albright](/wiki/Horace_M._Albright "Horace M. Albright") suggested that the NPS, rather than the [War Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_War "United States Department of War"), should manage historic [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") sites.[[15]](#cite_note-:5-15)

President Roosevelt agreed and issued two [executive orders](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order") to implement the reorganization. These two executive orders transferred to the NPS all of the War Department's historic sites as well as national monuments that the [Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture") had managed and parks in and around Washington, D.C. that an independent federal office had previously operated.[[16]](#cite_note-Shaping-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/HFCA_1607_NPS_1972_Centennial%2C_NBC_Today_Show_035.jpg_%2835a86faa2bbe433c831de71a4c03bb48%29.jpg/250px-HFCA_1607_NPS_1972_Centennial%2C_NBC_Today_Show_035.jpg_%2835a86faa2bbe433c831de71a4c03bb48%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HFCA_1607_NPS_1972_Centennial,_NBC_Today_Show_035.jpg_(35a86faa2bbe433c831de71a4c03bb48).jpg)

NPS staff sitting on the set for the 1972 Centennial for the creation of the first National Park (the [Yellowstone NP](/wiki/Yellowstone_NP "Yellowstone NP")), in a [NBC Today Show](/wiki/NBC_Today_Show "NBC Today Show"). Left to right: [George Hartzog](/wiki/George_B._Hartzog_Jr. "George B. Hartzog Jr."), William Everhart, [Frank McGee](/wiki/Frank_McGee_(journalist) "Frank McGee (journalist)") and Jack K. Anderson.

The popularity of the parks after the end of the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") left them overburdened with demands that the NPS could not meet. In 1951, [Conrad Wirth](/wiki/Conrad_Wirth "Conrad Wirth") became director of the NPS and began to bring park facilities up to the standards that the public was expecting.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 1952, with the support of President [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower"), Wirth began [Mission 66](/wiki/Mission_66 "Mission 66"), a ten-year effort to upgrade and expand park facilities for the 50th anniversary of the Park Service. New parks were added to preserve unique resources and existing park facilities were upgraded and expanded.[[16]](#cite_note-Shaping-16)

In 1966, as the Park Service turned 50 years old, emphasis began to turn from just saving great and wonderful scenery and unique natural features to making parks accessible to the public.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Director [George Hartzog](/wiki/George_B._Hartzog_Jr. "George B. Hartzog Jr.") began the process with the creation of the [National Lakeshores](/wiki/United_States_National_Lakeshore "United States National Lakeshore") and then [National Recreation Areas](/wiki/National_Recreation_Area "National Recreation Area").

### Resource stewardship policies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Resource stewardship policies")]

#### 1963: The Leopold Report

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1963: The Leopold Report")]

A 1963 report titled "Wildlife Management in the National Parks" was prepared by a five-member advisory board on Wildlife Management, appointed by [United States Secretary of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior") [Stewart Udall](/wiki/Stewart_Udall "Stewart Udall").[[19]](#cite_note-19) In later years, this report came to be referred to by its chairman and principal author, [A. Starker Leopold](/wiki/A._Starker_Leopold "A. Starker Leopold"). The [Leopold Report](/wiki/Leopold_Report "Leopold Report") was just fourteen pages in length, but it set forth [ecosystem management](/wiki/Ecosystem_management "Ecosystem management") recommendations that would guide parks policy until it was revisited in 2012.

The Leopold Report was the first concrete plan for managing park visitors and ecosystems under unified principles.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Park management issues and controversies addressed in this report included the difficulties of managing elk populations in [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") and how "overprotection from natural ground fires" in California's [Sequoia National Park](/wiki/Sequoia_National_Park "Sequoia National Park"), [Kings Canyon National Park](/wiki/Kings_Canyon_National_Park "Kings Canyon National Park"), and [Yosemite National Park](/wiki/Yosemite_National_Park "Yosemite National Park") had begun to threaten groves of [Giant Sequoia](/wiki/Giant_Sequoia "Giant Sequoia") with catastrophic wildfires. The report also established a historical baseline that read, "The goal of managing the national parks and monuments should be to preserve, or where necessary to recreate, the ecologic scene as viewed by the first European visitors." This baseline would guide [ecological restoration](/wiki/Ecological_restoration "Ecological restoration") in national parks until a [climate change adaptation](/wiki/Climate_change_adaptation "Climate change adaptation") policy, "Resist-Adapt-Direct", was later established in 2021.

#### 2012: Revisiting Leopold: Resource Stewardship in the National Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2012: Revisiting Leopold: Resource Stewardship in the National Parks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Revisiting_Leopold_NPS_Report.jpg/250px-Revisiting_Leopold_NPS_Report.jpg)](/wiki/File:Revisiting_Leopold_NPS_Report.jpg)

NPS publication, 2012

National Parks director [Jonathan Jarvis](/wiki/Jonathan_Jarvis "Jonathan Jarvis") charged the twelve-member NPS Advisory Board Science Committee to take a fresh look at the ecological issues and make recommendations for updating the original Leopold Report. The committee published their 23-page report in 2012, titled, "Revisiting Leopold: Resource Stewardship in the National Parks".[[21]](#cite_note-21) The report recommended that parks leadership "manage for change while confronting uncertainty."

> "... New and emerging scientific disciplines — including conservation biology, global change science, and genomics — along with new technological tools like high-resolution remote sensing can provide significant information for constructing contemporary tactics for NPS stewardship. This knowledge is essential to a National Park Service that is science-informed at all organizational levels and able to respond with contemporary strategies for resource management and ultimately park stewardship."

#### 2021: Resist–Accept–Direct (RAD): A Framework for the 21st-century Natural Resource Manager

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2021: Resist–Accept–Direct (RAD): A Framework for the 21st-century Natural Resource Manager")]

The "Revisiting Leopold" report mentioned [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") three times and "climate refugia" once, but it did not prescribe or offer any management tactics that could help park managers with the problems of climate change. Hence, the 2020 NPS-led report specific to the need for [climate adaptation](/wiki/Climate_adaptation "Climate adaptation"): "Resist–Accept–Direct (RAD): A Framework for the 21st-century Natural Resource Manager."[[22]](#cite_note-framework-2021-22) This "Natural Resource Report" has ten authors. Among them are four associated with the National Park Service, three with the [US Fish and Wildlife Service](/wiki/United_States_Fish_and_Wildlife_Service "United States Fish and Wildlife Service"), and two with the [US Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") — all of which are government agencies within the US Department of Interior.

The report's Executive Summary, points to "intensifying global change."

> "... The convention of using baseline conditions to define goals for today's resource management is increasingly untenable, presenting practical and philosophical challenges for managers. As formerly familiar ecological conditions continue to change, bringing novelty, surprise, and uncertainty, natural resource managers require a new, shared approach to make conservation decisions.... The RAD (Resist–Accept–Direct) decision framework has emerged over the past decade as a simple tool that captures the entire decision space for responding to ecosystems facing the potential for rapid, irreversible ecological change."[[22]](#cite_note-framework-2021-22)

The three RAD options are:

* **Resist** the trajectory, by working to maintain or restore ecosystem composition, structure, processes, or function on the basis of historical or acceptable current conditions;
* **Accept** the trajectory, by allowing ecosystem composition, structure, processes, or function to change autonomously; or
* **Direct** the trajectory, by actively shaping ecosystem composition, structure, processes, or function toward preferred new conditions.[[23]](#cite_note-BioScience-2022-23)

The RAD framework emerged from efforts by the NPS and partners since 2015 to hone a tool that could integrate into standard resource-management planning processes and thereby foster strategic thinking and clear communication about how to steward transforming ecosystems. It built on the Resist–Accept–Guide framework first proposed in the 2012 book *Beyond Naturalness: Rethinking Park and Wilderness Stewardship in an Era of Rapid Change.*[[24]](#cite_note-Beyond-Naturalness-24) The NPS and partners in 2021 replaced the 2012 term "guide" with "direct." This explicitly recognized the potential for strong intervention at key points to foster preferred new conditions. Initially, the NPS experimented with the term "accommodate" in place of "accept." This early formulation appeared in a 2016 NPS publication: *Coastal Adaptation Strategies Handbook*.[[25]](#cite_note-nps-coastal-2016-25) Another interagency publication in 2016 also used the term "accommodate": *Resource Management and Operations in Central North Dakota: Climate change scenario planning workshop summary*.[[26]](#cite_note-2016-usgs-26) In 2020, the "Resist-Accept-Direct" framework was used in a paper published in the journal *Fisheries*. Eighteen researchers from federal and state agencies and universities collaborated in this effort, which included short case studies of where and how this framework had already been applied.[[27]](#cite_note-2020-Fisheries-27)

The interagency efforts to forge a climate-adaptive framework culminated in a January 2022 series of six articles in the journal *[BioScience](/wiki/BioScience "BioScience")*. These were grouped in the "Special Section on the Resist–Accept–Direct Framework."[[28]](#cite_note-2022-special-section-28) In 2024, the RAD Framework was included in an NPS policy memorandum titled "Managing National Parks in an Era of Climate Change."[[29]](#cite_note-nps-24-03-29) That memorandum also links to the three previous statements pertaining to NPS climate change responses and adaptation (2012, 2014, and 2015).

**2025**

In 2025 the National Park Service (NPS) underwent a period of intense workforce contraction and political scrutiny during Donald Trump’s second term as president of the United States. On 14 February 2025, more than 1000 probationary and lower-seniority NPS employees were dismissed in an event widely referred to by staff and media as the "Valentine’s Day Massacre".[[30]](#cite_note-30) The cuts were followed by buyouts and early retirements for longer-serving rangers and specialists, as well as a federal hiring freeze that prevented most vacant positions from being refilled. Advocacy groups such as the National Parks Conservation Association (NPCA) reported that by mid-2025 the NPS had lost roughly 25% of its permanent workforce since January 2025.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

These conditions were further tested in October 2025, when a federal government shutdown began after Congress failed to pass appropriations legislation. The Interior Department’s contingency plan directed that most of the more than 400 NPS units remain at least partially open, while furloughing roughly 9,200–9,300 employees—about two-thirds of NPS staff—and keeping only limited "excepted" personnel such as law enforcement, emergency responders and dispatchers on duty.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Further information: [African-American Heritage Sites](/wiki/African-American_Heritage_Sites "African-American Heritage Sites"), [Hispanic Heritage Sites](/wiki/Hispanic_Heritage_Sites "Hispanic Heritage Sites"), [Native American Heritage Sites (National Park Service)](/wiki/Native_American_Heritage_Sites_(National_Park_Service) "Native American Heritage Sites (National Park Service)"), and [Women's history sites (National Park Service)](/wiki/Women%27s_history_sites_(National_Park_Service) "Women's history sites (National Park Service)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Grand_Canyon_South_Rim_Sunset.jpeg/250px-Grand_Canyon_South_Rim_Sunset.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Canyon_South_Rim_Sunset.jpeg)

[Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), south rim of canyon.

The National Park System includes all properties managed by the National Park Service, which have a wide variety of titles or designations. The system as a whole is considered to be a [national treasure](/wiki/National_treasure "National treasure") of the United States, and some of the more famous national parks and monuments are sometimes referred to as "[crown jewels](/wiki/Crown_jewels "Crown jewels")".[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The system encompasses approximately 85.1 million acres (0.344 million km2), of which 2.6 million acres (0.011 million km2) remain in private ownership. The largest unit is [Wrangell–St. Elias National Park and Preserve](/wiki/Wrangell-St._Elias_National_Park_and_Preserve "Wrangell-St. Elias National Park and Preserve"), Alaska. At 13,200,000 acres (53,000 km2), it is over 16 percent of the entire system. The smallest unit in the system is [Thaddeus Kosciuszko National Memorial](/wiki/Thaddeus_Kosciuszko_National_Memorial "Thaddeus Kosciuszko National Memorial"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), at 0.02 acres (80 m2).

In addition to administering its units and other properties, the NPS also provides technical and financial assistance to several affiliated areas authorized by Congress. The largest affiliated area is [New Jersey Pinelands National Reserve](/wiki/New_Jersey_Pinelands_National_Reserve "New Jersey Pinelands National Reserve") at 1,164,025 acres (4711 km2). The smallest is [Benjamin Franklin National Memorial](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_National_Memorial "Benjamin Franklin National Memorial") at less than 0.01 acres (40 m2).

While there are laws generally covering all units of the National Park System, they are subject to management policies of individual pieces of authorizing legislation or, in the case of national monuments created under the [Antiquities Act](/wiki/Antiquities_Act "Antiquities Act"), [Executive Order](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order"). For example, because of provisions within their enabling legislation, [Congaree National Park](/wiki/Congaree_National_Park "Congaree National Park") is almost entirely a [wilderness area](/wiki/National_Wilderness_Preservation_System "National Wilderness Preservation System") devoid of development, yet Yosemite allows unique developments such as the [Badger Pass Ski Area](/wiki/Badger_Pass_Ski_Area "Badger Pass Ski Area") and the [O'Shaughnessy Dam](/wiki/Hetch_Hetchy "Hetch Hetchy") within its boundaries. Such irregularities would not be found in other parks unless specifically provided for with exceptions by the legislation that created them.

### Criteria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Criteria")]

Most NPS units have been established by an act of Congress, with the president confirming the action by signing the act into law. The exception, under the [Antiquities Act](/wiki/Antiquities_Act "Antiquities Act"), allows the president to designate and protect areas as national monuments by executive order. Regardless of the method used, all parks are to be of national importance.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

A potential park should meet all four of the following standards:[[36]](#cite_note-36)

* It is an outstanding example of a particular type of resource.
* It possesses exceptional value or quality in illustrating or interpreting the natural or cultural themes of the nation's heritage.
* It offers superlative opportunities for recreation, for public use and enjoyment, or for scientific study.
* It retains a high degree of integrity as a true, accurate, and relatively unspoiled example of the resource.

Before creation of a new unit, Congress typically directs the NPS to conduct a special resource study of a site to determine its national significance and suitability to be part of the National Park System.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

**[National preserves](/wiki/National_preserve "National preserve")** are for the protection of certain resources and operate similar to many National Parks, but allow limited resource extraction. Activities like hunting, fishing, and some mining may be allowed depending on the site. [Big Cypress National Preserve](/wiki/Big_Cypress_National_Preserve "Big Cypress National Preserve") and [Big Thicket National Preserve](/wiki/Big_Thicket "Big Thicket") were created in 1974 as the first national preserves.

**National reserves** are similar to national preserves, but the operational authority can be placed with a state or local government. [New Jersey Pinelands National Reserve](/wiki/New_Jersey_Pinelands_National_Reserve "New Jersey Pinelands National Reserve") was the first to be established in 1978.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Winter at the [Gettysburg Battlefield](/wiki/Gettysburg_National_Military_Park "Gettysburg National Military Park")

[**National military parks**, **battlefield parks**, **battlefield sites**, and **battlefields**](/wiki/National_Military_Park "National Military Park") preserve areas associated with military history. The different designations reflect the complexity of the event and the site. Many of the sites preserve important Revolutionary War battles and Civil War battlefields. *Military parks* are the sites of larger actions, such as [Chickamauga and Chattanooga National Military Park](/wiki/Chickamauga_and_Chattanooga_National_Military_Park "Chickamauga and Chattanooga National Military Park"), [Vicksburg National Military Park](/wiki/Vicksburg_National_Military_Park "Vicksburg National Military Park"), [Gettysburg National Military Park](/wiki/Gettysburg_National_Military_Park "Gettysburg National Military Park"), and [Shiloh National Military Park](/wiki/Shiloh_National_Military_Park "Shiloh National Military Park")—the original four from 1890.

Examples of *battlefield parks*, *battlefield sites*, and *national battlefields* include [Richmond National Battlefield Park](/wiki/Richmond_National_Battlefield_Park "Richmond National Battlefield Park"), [Brices Cross Roads National Battlefield Site](/wiki/Brices_Cross_Roads_National_Battlefield_Site "Brices Cross Roads National Battlefield Site"), and [Antietam National Battlefield](/wiki/Antietam_National_Battlefield "Antietam National Battlefield").

[**National memorials**](/wiki/List_of_national_memorials_of_the_United_States "List of national memorials of the United States") are areas that officially memorialize a person or event, though unlike a National Historical Site, may or may not be placed at a specific historical location. Several national memorials are on the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall"), such as the [Washington Monument](/wiki/Washington_Monument "Washington Monument") and [Lincoln Memorial](/wiki/Lincoln_Memorial "Lincoln Memorial").

[**National seashores** and **national lakeshores**](/wiki/List_of_United_States_national_lakeshores_and_seashores "List of United States national lakeshores and seashores") offer preservation of the national coast line, while supporting water–based recreation. [Cape Hatteras National Seashore](/wiki/Cape_Hatteras_National_Seashore "Cape Hatteras National Seashore") was created in 1937. [Indiana Dunes National Lakeshore](/wiki/Indiana_Dunes_National_Lakeshore "Indiana Dunes National Lakeshore") and [Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore](/wiki/Pictured_Rocks_National_Lakeshore "Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore"), created in 1966, were the first national lakeshores.

[**National rivers** and **wild and scenic riverways**](/wiki/National_Wild_and_Scenic_Rivers_System "National Wild and Scenic Rivers System") protect free-flowing streams over their length. The riverways may not be altered with dams, channelization, or other changes. Recreational pursuits are encouraged along the waterways. [Ozark National Scenic Riverways](/wiki/Ozark_National_Scenic_Riverways "Ozark National Scenic Riverways") was established in 1964.

The **[National Trails System](/wiki/National_Trails_System "National Trails System")** preserves long-distance routes across America. The system was created in 1968 and consists of two major components: **National scenic trails** are long-distance trails through some of the most scenic parts of the country. They received official protection in 1968. The [Appalachian Trail](/wiki/Appalachian_Trail "Appalachian Trail") is the best known. **National historic trails** commemorate the routes of major historic events. Some of the best known are the [Trail of Tears](/wiki/Trail_of_Tears "Trail of Tears"), the [Mormon Trail](/wiki/Mormon_Trail "Mormon Trail"), and the [Santa Fe Trail](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Trail "Santa Fe Trail"). These trails are administered by several federal agencies.

### Special designations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Special designations")]

[Wilderness](/wiki/Wilderness "Wilderness") areas are part of the [National Wilderness Preservation System](/wiki/National_Wilderness_Preservation_System "National Wilderness Preservation System"), which consists of federally managed lands that are of a pristine condition, established by the [Wilderness Act](/wiki/Wilderness_Act "Wilderness Act") (Public Law 88-577) in 1964. The National Wilderness Preservation System originally created hundreds of [wilderness zones](/wiki/List_of_wilderness_areas_of_the_United_States "List of wilderness areas of the United States") within already protected federally administered property, consisting of over 9 million acres (36,000 km2).

[Marine Protected Areas](/wiki/Marine_Protected_Area "Marine Protected Area") (MPAs) began with Executive Order 13158 in May 2000, when official MPAs were established for the first time.[[44]](#cite_note-FR75100-44) The initial listing of U.S. areas was presented in 2010, consisting of areas already set aside under other legislation. The NPS has 19 park units designated as MPAs.[[44]](#cite_note-FR75100-44)

## Visitation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Visitation")]

The National Park System received over 325 million recreation visits in 2023.[[42]](#cite_note-:3-42) Park visitation grew 64 percent between 1979 and 2015.[[45]](#cite_note-NPS_Reports-45) In 2024, NPS reported a record 331.9 million recreation visits.[[46]](#cite_note-irma24-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The 10 most-visited units of the National Park System handle around 30 percent of the overall visits. The top 10 percent of parks (43) handle over 64 percent of all visits, leaving the remaining more than 380 units to accommodate around 36 percent of visits.[[42]](#cite_note-:3-42) (Note that only 380 sites recorded visitors during 2021 due to COVID-19-related closures).[[45]](#cite_note-NPS_Reports-45)

### Entrance fees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Entrance fees")]

Main article: [List of fee areas in the United States National Park System](/wiki/List_of_fee_areas_in_the_United_States_National_Park_System "List of fee areas in the United States National Park System")

Most areas of the National Park System do not charge entrance fees and are completely supported by tax dollars, although some of the most popular areas do charge entrance fees. Fees vary site to site and are charged either on a per-vehicle or per-person basis, with most passes valid for 7 days. The [America the Beautiful Pass](/wiki/America_the_Beautiful_Pass "America the Beautiful Pass") series waives the per-vehicle fee or per-person fee for the holder and up to 3 other adults (children age 15 and younger are admitted for free at most sites). Annual passes for single areas are also available for those who visit the same site often.

### Overnight stays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Overnight stays")]

Over 15 million visitors spent a night in one of the national park units during 2015. The largest number (3.68 million) were tent campers. The second largest group (3.38 million) stayed in one of the lodges, followed by miscellaneous stays (on boats, group sites—2.15 million). The last three groups of over-night visitors included RV campers (2.26 million), backcountry campers (2.02 million) and users of the concession-run campgrounds (1.42 million).[[51]](#cite_note-Stats-53)

## Budget

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Budget")]

See also: [United States federal budget](/wiki/United_States_federal_budget "United States federal budget") and [United States budget process](/wiki/United_States_budget_process "United States budget process")

In 2019, the NPS had an annual budget of $4.085 billion and an estimated $12 billion maintenance backlog.[[52]](#cite_note-54) On August 4, 2020, the [Great American Outdoors Act](/wiki/Great_American_Outdoors_Act "Great American Outdoors Act") was signed into law reducing the $12 billion maintenance backlog by $9.5 billion over a 5-year period beginning in FY 2021.[[53]](#cite_note-55) As of 2022, the NPS had the largest budget allocation of any [Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior") bureau or program.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

The NPS budget is divided into two primary areas, *discretionary* and *mandatory* spending. Within each of these areas, there are numerous specific purposes to which Congress directs the services activities.[[55]](#cite_note-FY2006-57)

The NPS budget includes *discretionary* spending which is broken out into two portions: the direct operations of the National Parks and the special initiatives.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58) Listed separately are the special initiatives of the service for the year specified in the legislation. During fiscal year 2010, the service was charged with five initiatives. They include: stewardship and education; professional excellence; youth programs; climate change impacts; and budget restructure and realignment.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58)

### Discretionary spending

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Discretionary spending")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/NPS_Budget_%282001-2006%29.jpg/250px-NPS_Budget_%282001-2006%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:NPS_Budget_(2001-2006).jpg)

NPS *Operations of the National Parks* budget from FY 2001-FY 2006

Discretionary spending includes the Operations of the National Parks (ONPS), from which all park operations are paid. The [United States Park Police](/wiki/United_States_Park_Police "United States Park Police") funds cover the high-profile law enforcement operations at some of the large parks, including [Gateway National Recreation Area](/wiki/Gateway_National_Recreation_Area "Gateway National Recreation Area"), [Golden Gate National Recreation Area](/wiki/Golden_Gate_National_Recreation_Area "Golden Gate National Recreation Area"), and the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall"). The *National Recreation and Preservation Program* and the *Urban Park and Recreation Fund* are outreach programs to support state and local outdoor recreational activities.[[55]](#cite_note-FY2006-57)

The ONPS section of the budget is divided into six operational areas. These areas include:

#### Resource stewardship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Resource stewardship")]

These are funds and people directed towards the restoration, preservation, and maintenance of natural and cultural resources. The resource staff includes biologists, geologists, archeologists, museum curators, preservation specialists, and a variety of specialized employees to restore and preserve cultural buildings or natural features.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58)

#### Visitor services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Visitor services")]

The NPS allocates funds obtained from its visitor services for use in public programs and for educational programs for the general public and school groups. Park rangers trained in providing walks, talks, and educational programs to the public frequently conduct such programs. Media specialists prepare exhibits along trails, roads and in visitor contact facilities, as well as written brochures and web-sites.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58)

#### Park protection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Park protection")]

This includes the staff responding to visitor emergencies (criminal, medical, search and rescue), and the protection of the park's natural and cultural resources from damage by those persons visiting the park. The staff includes [law enforcement rangers](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Law_Enforcement_Rangers "National Park Service Law Enforcement Rangers"), [park police](/wiki/United_States_Park_Police "United States Park Police"), lifeguards, criminal investigators, and communication center operators.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58) In many instances they also work with [state and territorial fish and wildlife management agency rangers](/wiki/List_of_state_and_territorial_fish_and_wildlife_management_agencies_in_the_United_States "List of state and territorial fish and wildlife management agencies in the United States").

#### Facility maintenance and operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Facility maintenance and operations")]

This is the cost of maintaining the necessary infrastructure within each park that supports all the services provided. It includes the plows and heavy equipment for road clearing, repairs and construction. There are buildings, trails, roads, docks, boats, utility pipes and wires, and a variety of hidden systems that make a park accessible by the public. The staff includes equipment operators, custodians, trail crews, electricians, plumbers, engineers, architects, and other building trade specialists.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58)

#### Park support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Park support")]

This is the staff that provides for the routine logistical needs of the parks. There are human resource specialists, contracting officers, property specialists, budget managers, accountants and information technology specialists.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58)

#### External administrative costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: External administrative costs")]

The NPS pays external administrative costs to outside organizations that provide the logistical support that the NPS needs to operate its facilities. These costs include rent payments to the [General Services Administration](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration") for building space, postage payments to the postal machine vendor and other direct payments.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58)

### Land and Water Conservation Fund

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Land and Water Conservation Fund")]

The [Land and Water Conservation Fund](/wiki/Land_and_Water_Conservation_Fund "Land and Water Conservation Fund") (LWCF) supports Land Acquisition and State Conservation Assistance (SCA) grant programs. In 2010, the LWCF began an incremental process to fully fund its programs at a total cost of $900 million. The Department of the Interior and the [United States Forest Service](/wiki/United_States_Forest_Service "United States Forest Service") use these funds to purchase critical lands to protect existing public lands.

The LWCF also issues grants to States and local jurisdictions to preserve and protect Civil War battlefield sites that are not part of the national park system. The SCA program distributes funds for land preservation to individual states.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58)

### Historic Preservation Fund

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Historic Preservation Fund")]

The National Historic Preservation Act of 1966 set the federal vision for historic preservation in the United States. To support the vision and framework laid out in this act, the Historic Preservation Fund (HPF) was established in 1977 to provide financial assistance to, originally, states, to carry out activities related to preservation. Funding is provided from Outer Continental Shelf oil and gas lease revenues, not tax dollars, and an amount is appropriated annually by Congress. Awards from the HPF are made to States, Tribes, Territories, local governments, and non-profits.[[58]](#cite_note-60) Two specific programs include the [Save America's Treasures](/wiki/Save_America%27s_Treasures "Save America's Treasures") and the [Preserve America](/wiki/Preserve_America "Preserve America"). The Historic Preservation Offices makes grants available to the States, territories, and tribal lands.[[56]](#cite_note-FY2010-58) To honor the 250th anniversary of the United States, Congress authorized the Semiquincentennial Grant in 2020 to support the preservation of State owned sites and structures listed on the National Register of Historic Places that commemorate the founding of the nation.[[59]](#cite_note-61)

### Economic benefits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Economic benefits")]

The NPS affects economies at national, state, and local levels. According to a 2011 Michigan State University report prepared for the NPS, for each $1 invested in the NPS, the American public receives $4 in economic value.[[60]](#cite_note-62) In 2011, national parks generated $30.1 billion in economic activity and 252,000 jobs nationwide. Thirteen billion of that amount went directly into communities within 60 miles of a NPS unit.

In a 2017 study, the NPS found that 331 million park visitors spent $18.2 billion in local areas around National Parks across the nation. This spending helped support 306,000 jobs. The NPS expenditures supported $297 million in economic output in Missouri alone.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

Despite these economic advantages in February 2025 in an effort to decrease federal spending over 1,000 NPS employees were fired, leaving many sites grossly understaffed.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

## Concessions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Concessions")]

In an effort to increase visitation and allow for a larger audience to enjoy national park land, the NPS has numerous [concession](/wiki/Concession_(contract) "Concession (contract)") contracts with private businesses to bring recreation, resorts and other *compatible* amenities to their parks. NPS lodging opportunities exist at places such as the [Wawona Hotel](/wiki/Wawona_Hotel "Wawona Hotel") in [Yosemite National Park](/wiki/Yosemite_National_Park "Yosemite National Park") and the [Fort Baker](/wiki/Fort_Baker "Fort Baker") Retreat and Conference Center in [Golden Gate National Recreation Area](/wiki/Golden_Gate_National_Recreation_Area "Golden Gate National Recreation Area").

* [Delaware North Corporation](/wiki/Delaware_North "Delaware North") at [Yosemite National Park](/wiki/Yosemite_National_Park "Yosemite National Park"), [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park"), South Rim [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park").
* [Forever Resorts](/wiki/Forever_Resorts "Forever Resorts") at [Big Bend National Park](/wiki/Big_Bend_National_Park "Big Bend National Park"), [Blue Ridge Parkway](/wiki/Blue_Ridge_Parkway "Blue Ridge Parkway"), [Badlands National Park](/wiki/Badlands_National_Park "Badlands National Park"), North Rim of [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), [Olympic National Park](/wiki/Olympic_National_Park "Olympic National Park"), [Lake Mead National Recreation Area](/wiki/Lake_Mead_National_Recreation_Area "Lake Mead National Recreation Area"), [Mammoth Cave National Park](/wiki/Mammoth_Cave_National_Park "Mammoth Cave National Park"), [Isle Royale National Park](/wiki/Isle_Royale_National_Park "Isle Royale National Park"), and [Rocky Mountain National Park](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_National_Park "Rocky Mountain National Park").
* [Xanterra Parks & Resorts](/wiki/Xanterra_Travel_Collection "Xanterra Travel Collection") at [Bryce Canyon National Park](/wiki/Bryce_Canyon_National_Park "Bryce Canyon National Park"), [Crater Lake National Park](/wiki/Crater_Lake_National_Park "Crater Lake National Park"), [Death Valley National Park](/wiki/Death_Valley_National_Park "Death Valley National Park"), South Rim [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), [Mount Rushmore National Memorial](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore_National_Memorial "Mount Rushmore National Memorial"), Painted Desert at [Petrified Forest National Park](/wiki/Petrified_Forest_National_Park "Petrified Forest National Park"), [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park"), and [Zion National Park](/wiki/Zion_National_Park "Zion National Park").

### Litigation with Delaware North

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Litigation with Delaware North")]

In 2015, [Delaware North](/wiki/Delaware_North "Delaware North") sued the NPS in the [United States Court of Federal Claims](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Federal_Claims "United States Court of Federal Claims") for breach of contract, alleging that the NPS had undervalued its trademarks of the names of iconic Yosemite National Park concession facilities.[[63]](#cite_note-Buffalo-65) The NPS estimated the value of the intangible assets including the names "Ahwahnee", "Badger Pass", "Curry Village", and "Yosemite Lodge" at $3.5 million. Delaware North lost the contract, and asserted that the historic names were worth $51 million and maintained that the incoming concessioner had to be paid that amount.[[64]](#cite_note-FresnoBee1-66)

The Justice Department and the NPS asserted that this was an "improper and wildly inflated" value. Rather than pay Delaware North's demanded valuation, in January 2016 the NPS instead opted to rename the famous landmarks, effective in March. The [Ahwahnee Hotel](/wiki/Ahwahnee_Hotel "Ahwahnee Hotel") is slated to become The Majestic Yosemite Hotel, Curry Village will become Half Dome Village, and the Wawona Hotel will become Big Trees Lodge.[[65]](#cite_note-FresnoBee2-67) Widespread public outcry focused on Delaware North's decision to claim ownership of names within a national park.[[66]](#cite_note-SacBeeQuestions-68) The names were restored in 2019 upon settlement of the dispute.[[67]](#cite_note-LAT_20190715-69)

## Offices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Offices")]

Main article: [Organization of the National Park Service](/wiki/Organization_of_the_National_Park_Service "Organization of the National Park Service")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/NPS_Employees_12_types_of_work_08368.jpg/250px-NPS_Employees_12_types_of_work_08368.jpg)](/wiki/File:NPS_Employees_12_types_of_work_08368.jpg)

Depicts twelve figures, most in NPS uniforms, shown in occupations from left to right: a lifeguard, a Civil War reenactor, fire management, mounted patrol, researcher and/or natural resources with fish, a female ranger with two visitors, a laborer, a climber/rescuer, and a youth with a male ranger.

The national headquarters is located in the [Main Interior Building](/wiki/Main_Interior_Building "Main Interior Building"), 1849 C Street NW, several blocks southwest of the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"). The central office is composed of eleven directorates: director/deputy directors; business services; workforce management; chief information officer; cultural resources; natural resource stewardship and science; office of the comptroller; park planning, facilities and lands; partnerships and visitor experience; visitor and resource protection; and the [United States Park Police](/wiki/United_States_Park_Police "United States Park Police").[[68]](#cite_note-70)

Regional offices are in [Anchorage](/wiki/Anchorage,_Alaska "Anchorage, Alaska"), [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), [Lakewood, CO](/wiki/Lakewood,_Colorado "Lakewood, Colorado") ([Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver")), [Omaha, NE](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area") and [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"). The headquarters building of the [National Park Service Southwest Regional Office](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Southwest_Regional_Office "National Park Service Southwest Regional Office") is architecturally significant and is designated a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark").

The NPS is an [operating unit of the U.S. Department of the Interior](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_the_Interior#operating_unit "U.S. Department of the Interior"). The [NPS director](/wiki/Director_of_the_National_Park_Service "Director of the National Park Service") is nominated by the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and confirmed by the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate").[[69]](#cite_note-71) The director is supported by six senior executives.

These executives manage national programs, policy, and budget from the Washington, DC, headquarters. Under the deputy director of operations are seven regional directors, who are responsible for national park management and program implementation. Together this group is called the *National Leadership Council*.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

### Employees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Employees")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/NPS_Staffing%281998-2009%29.JPG/250px-NPS_Staffing%281998-2009%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:NPS_Staffing(1998-2009).JPG)

National Park Service employment levels. Executives: abt 27; Gen Sch: 16–17,000; Others: 6–7,000[[71]](#cite_note-73)

By the mid-1950s, the primary employees of the service were the park rangers, who had broad responsibilities on the parks' behalf. They cleaned up trash, operated heavy equipment, fought fires, managed traffic, cleared trails and roads, provided information to visitors, managed museums, performed rescues, flew aircraft, and investigated crime.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

The NPS employs many kinds of workers:

* [National Park Service Ranger](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Ranger "National Park Service Ranger")
  + Interpreter
  + [Law enforcement](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Law_Enforcement_Rangers "National Park Service Law Enforcement Rangers")
* Park management (Superintendent/Deputy)
* [United States Park Police](/wiki/United_States_Park_Police "United States Park Police")
* Emergency management (Emergency medical providers, search and rescue specialists)
  + Lifeguards
* [Dispatchers](/wiki/Dispatcher "Dispatcher")
* Maintenance (including [carpenters](/wiki/Carpentry "Carpentry"), [plumbers](/wiki/Plumbing "Plumbing"), masons, laborers, auto mechanics, motor vehicle operators, [heavy equipment operators](/wiki/Heavy_equipment_operator "Heavy equipment operator"), [electricians](/wiki/Electrician "Electrician"))
* Park planning
  + [Architects](/wiki/Architect "Architect"), [engineers](/wiki/Engineer "Engineer"), and [landscape architects](/wiki/Landscape_architect "Landscape architect")
* Resource management (including archeologist, [biologist](/wiki/Biologist "Biologist"), [botanist](/wiki/Botanist "Botanist"), aquatics, soil scientist, [geologist](/wiki/Geologist "Geologist"))
* History ([curators](/wiki/Curator "Curator"), [historians](/wiki/Historian "Historian"), preservation technicians, historic architects, [archivists](/wiki/Archivist "Archivist"))
* Fire management (managers, weather specialist, [firefighters](/wiki/Firefighter "Firefighter"), engine captains, crew superintendents, battalion chiefs)
* Public affairs
* Administration (human resources, finance, accountants, information technology, budgeting, concessions management)

Locations are varied. Parks exist in the nation's larger cities like [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") ([Federal Hall](/wiki/Federal_Hall "Federal Hall") Memorial National Historic Site), [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") ([Martin Luther King, Jr. National Historic Site](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King,_Jr._National_Historic_Site "Martin Luther King, Jr. National Historic Site")), and [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego") ([Cabrillo National Monument](/wiki/Cabrillo_National_Monument "Cabrillo National Monument")) to some of the remotest areas of the continent like [Hovenweep National Monument](/wiki/Hovenweep_National_Monument "Hovenweep National Monument") in southeastern Utah, to [Aniakchak National Monument](/wiki/Aniakchak_National_Monument "Aniakchak National Monument") in [King Salmon, Alaska](/wiki/King_Salmon,_Alaska "King Salmon, Alaska").[[73]](#cite_note-75)

### Volunteers-In-Parks (VIP)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Volunteers-In-Parks (VIP)")]

The [Volunteers-In-Parks](/wiki/Volunteers-In-Parks "Volunteers-In-Parks") program was authorized in 1969 by the Volunteers in the Parks Act of 1969.[[74]](#cite_note-05Report-76) for the purpose of allowing the public to serve in the nations parks providing support and skills for their enhancement and protection.[[75]](#cite_note-brochure-77)

Volunteers come from all walks of life and include professionals, artists, laborers, homemakers and students, performing varied duties. Many come from surrounding communities and some travel significant distances.[[75]](#cite_note-brochure-77) In a 2005 annual report, the NPS reported that,

> ...137,000 VIPs contributed 5.2 million hours of service (or 2500 FTEs) valued at $91,260,000 based on the private sector value figure of $17.55 as used by AARP, Points of Light Foundation, and other large-scale volunteer programs including many federal agencies. There are 365 separate volunteer programs throughout the NPS. Since 1990, the number of volunteers has increased an average of 2% per year.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

FTE stands for full-time equivalent (one work year). In 2012, the National Park Service reported that over 221,000 volunteers contributed about 6.4 million hours annually.[[77]](#cite_note-nps.gov-79)

Additionally, other types of volunteers also conduct offsite NPS public outreach and education, such as the Trails & Rails program guides on board certain segments of long-haul [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") routes, who offer passengers insights to the travel area's natural resources and heritage.[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81)

### Artist-In-Residence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Artist-In-Residence")]

Across the nation, there are special opportunities for artists (visual artists, photographers, sculptors, performers, writers, composers, and crafts) to live and work in a park. Twenty-nine parks currently participate in the *Artist-In-Residence program*.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

### United States Park Rangers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: United States Park Rangers")]

Main article: [National Park Service ranger](/wiki/National_Park_Service_ranger "National Park Service ranger")

See also: [National Park Service Law Enforcement Rangers](/wiki/National_Park_Service_Law_Enforcement_Rangers "National Park Service Law Enforcement Rangers")

**National Park Service rangers** are among the uniformed employees charged with protecting and preserving areas set aside in the National Park System by the United States Congress and the President of the United States. While all employees of the agency contribute to the National Park Service mission of preserving unimpaired the natural and cultural resources set aside by the American people for [future generations](/wiki/Future_generations "Future generations"), the term "[park ranger](/wiki/Park_ranger "Park ranger")" is traditionally used to describe all National Park Service employees who wear the uniform. Broadly speaking, all National Park Service rangers promote [stewardship](/wiki/Stewardship "Stewardship") of the resources in their care—either voluntary stewardship via [resource interpretation](/wiki/Heritage_interpretation "Heritage interpretation"), or compliance with [statute](/wiki/Statute "Statute") or [regulation](/wiki/Regulation "Regulation") through [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement"). These comprise the two main disciplines of the ranger profession in the National Park Service.

Law enforcement rangers, or protection rangers, are uniformed federal law enforcement officers with broad authority to enforce federal and state laws within NPS sites. The NPS commonly refers to law enforcement operations in the agency as *visitor and resource protection*.

In most NPS units, law enforcement rangers are the primary police agency.[[81]](#cite_note-83) The NPS also employs special agents who conduct more complex criminal investigations. Rangers and agents receive extensive police training at the [Federal Law Enforcement Training Center](/wiki/Federal_Law_Enforcement_Training_Center "Federal Law Enforcement Training Center") and annual in-service and regular firearms training.

### United States Park Police

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: United States Park Police")]

Main article: [United States Park Police](/wiki/United_States_Park_Police "United States Park Police")

The United States Park Police (USPP) is the oldest uniformed federal law enforcement agency in the United States. It functions as a full service law enforcement agency with responsibilities and jurisdiction in those NPS areas primarily located in the Washington, D.C. Parks, San Francisco, and [New York City Parks](/wiki/New_York_City_Parks "New York City Parks") areas.

In addition to performing the normal crime prevention, investigation, and apprehension functions of an urban police force, the park police are responsible for policing many of the famous monuments in the United States and share law enforcement jurisdiction in all lands administered by the service with a force of national park rangers tasked with the same law enforcement powers and responsibilities.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

### Youth programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Youth programs")]

The NPS partners with a variety of youth oriented programs. The oldest serving group is the [Student Conservation Association](/wiki/Student_Conservation_Association "Student Conservation Association") (SCA). It was established in 1957, committed to conservation and preservation. The SCA's goal is to create the next generation of conservation leaders.

SCA volunteers work through internships, conservation jobs, and crew experiences. Volunteers conduct resource management, historic preservation, cultural resources and conservation programs to gain experience, which can lead to career development and further educational opportunities. The SCA places volunteers in more than 350 national park units and NPS offices each year.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

The Corps Network, formerly known as the National Association for Service and Corps (NASCC), represents 136 Service and Conservation Corps. These groups have programs in 42 states and the District of Columbia. Corpsmembers are between the ages of 16–25. Service and Conservation Corps are direct descendants of the [Civilian Conservation Corps](/wiki/Civilian_Conservation_Corps "Civilian Conservation Corps") (CCC) of the 1930s that built park facilities in the national parks and other public parks around the country. The Corps Network was established in 1985.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

* The Youth Conservation Corps (ages 15–18) brings young people into a park to restore, preserve and protect a natural, cultural, or historical resources. Enrollees are paid for their work.[[85]](#cite_note-87)
* [Public Land Corps](/wiki/Public_Land_Corps "Public Land Corps") (ages 16–25) is a job helping to restore, protect, and rehabilitate a local national parks. The enrollees learn about environmental issues and the parks.[[86]](#cite_note-88)
