### Centers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Centers")]

The NPS operates four archaeology-related centers: Harpers Ferry Center, in Harpers Ferry, West Virginia; the Midwest Archeological Center, in Lincoln, Nebraska; the Southeast Archeological Center, in Tallahassee, Florida; and the Western Archeological and Conservation Center, in Tucson, Arizona. The Harpers Ferry Center specializes in developing interpretive media and in conserving objects. The others focus to various degrees on archaeological research and the curation and conservation of museum objects.

[National Park Service training centers](/wiki/National_Park_Service_training_centers "National Park Service training centers") include the Horace Albright Training Center, Grand Canyon; the Stephen Mather Training Center, Harpers Ferry, West Virginia; the Historic Preservation Training Center, Frederick, Maryland; and the Capital Training Center, Washington, D.C.

The [Submerged Resources Center](/wiki/Submerged_Resources_Center "Submerged Resources Center") catalogues and evaluates submerged resources in the National Park system.[[98]](#cite_note-RRR10261-100) The SRC's headquarters are at the Intermountain Region's headquarters, in Lakewood, Colorado.[[98]](#cite_note-RRR10261-100)

The [National Center for Preservation Technology and Training](/wiki/National_Center_for_Preservation_Technology_and_Training "National Center for Preservation Technology and Training"), in Natchitoches, Louisiana, conducts research and training in archaeology, architecture, landscape architecture, and materials conservation.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

### Preservation Programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Preservation Programs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/El_Santuario_del_Senor_Esquipula_113804pu.jpg/250px-El_Santuario_del_Senor_Esquipula_113804pu.jpg)](/wiki/File:El_Santuario_del_Senor_Esquipula_113804pu.jpg)

Photograph of El Santuario Del Señor Esquipula, Chimayo, New Mexico

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Chicago_River_Bascule_Bridge%2C_LaSalle_Street%2C_Chicago.jpg/250px-Chicago_River_Bascule_Bridge%2C_LaSalle_Street%2C_Chicago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_River_Bascule_Bridge,_LaSalle_Street,_Chicago.jpg)

LaSalle Street Bridge, Chicago, Illinois

The oldest federal preservation program, the [Historic American Buildings Survey](/wiki/Historic_American_Buildings_Survey "Historic American Buildings Survey")/[Historic American Engineering Record](/wiki/Historic_American_Engineering_Record "Historic American Engineering Record") (HABS/HAER), produces graphic and written documentation of historically significant architectural, engineering and industrial sites and structures. Dating from 1934, the [Historic American Buildings Survey](/wiki/Historic_American_Buildings_Survey "Historic American Buildings Survey") (HABS) was chartered to document historic architecture—primarily houses and public buildings—of national or regional significance. Originally a [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") employment/preservation program, after World War II, HABS employed summer teams of advanced undergraduate and graduate students to carry out the documentation, a tradition followed to this day. Many of the structures they documented no longer exist.

HABS/HAER produces measured drawings, large-format photographs and written histories of historic sites, structures and objects, that are significant to the architectural, engineering and industrial heritage of the U.S. Its 25,000 records are part of the Library of Congress. HABS/HAER is administered by the NPS Washington office and five regional offices.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

#### Historic American Buildings Survey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Historic American Buildings Survey")]

In 1933, the NPS established the Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS), based on a proposal by Charles E. Peterson, Park Service landscape architect. It was founded as a make-work program for architects, draftsmen and photographers left jobless by the Great Depression. Guided by field instructions from Washington, D.C., the first recorders were tasked with documenting a representative sampling of America's architectural heritage. After 70 years, there is now an archive of historic architecture. HABS provided a database of primary source material for the then fledgling historic preservation movement.

#### Historic American Engineering Record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Historic American Engineering Record")]

Recognizing a similar fragility in the national industrial and engineering heritage, the NPS, the Library of Congress and the American Society of Civil Engineers (ASCE) formed the HAER program in 1969, to document nationally and regionally significant engineering and industrial sites. Later, HAER was ratified by the [American Society of Mechanical Engineers](/wiki/American_Society_of_Mechanical_Engineers "American Society of Mechanical Engineers") (ASME), the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), the [American Institute of Chemical Engineers](/wiki/American_Institute_of_Chemical_Engineers "American Institute of Chemical Engineers") (AIChE) and the American Institute of Mining, Metallurgical and Petroleum Engineers (AIME). HAER documentation, in the forms of measured and interpretive drawings, large-format photographs and written histories, is archivally preserved in the Prints and Photographs Division of the Library of Congress, where it is readily available to the public.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

**Historic American Landscapes Survey**

With the growing vitality of landscape history, preservation and management, proper recognition for historic American landscape documentation must be addressed. In response to this need, the [American Society of Landscape Architects](/wiki/American_Society_of_Landscape_Architects "American Society of Landscape Architects") Historic Preservation Professional Interest Group worked with the National Park Service to establish a national program. Hence, in October 2000 the National Park Service permanently established the [Historic American Landscapes Survey](/wiki/Historic_American_Landscapes_Survey "Historic American Landscapes Survey") (HALS) program for the systematic documentation of historic American landscapes.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

#### Rivers, Trails and Conservation Assistance Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Rivers, Trails and Conservation Assistance Program")]

The NPS Rivers, Trails and Conservation Assistance (NPS-RTCA) program is designed to assist local communities and the public with planning for conservation and outdoor recreation projects.[[103]](#cite_note-105) The NPS-RTCA program is able to work with local communities outside the borders of the nation's National Parks because of the second sentence of the NPS Mission Statement.[[104]](#cite_note-106) Unlike the mainline National Park Programs, these programs take place on non-federal property at the request of the local community. One of their better known programs is [Rails to Trails](/wiki/Rails_to_Trails "Rails to Trails"), where unused railroad right-of-ways are converted into public hiking and biking trails.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

#### Japanese American Confinement Sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Japanese American Confinement Sites")]

The National Park Service is responsible for the management and upkeep of several sites where [Americans of Japanese descent](/wiki/Japanese_Americans "Japanese Americans") were [forcibly relocated and incarcerated Japanese Americans](/wiki/Internment_of_Japanese_Americans "Internment of Japanese Americans") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") between 1942 and 1946 under the order of President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"). The [Japanese American Confinement Sites](/wiki/List_of_Japanese_American_Confinement_Sites "List of Japanese American Confinement Sites") (JACS) grant program provides funding for applicants that preserve these sites and their memory.[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109)

### National Trails System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: National Trails System")]

The [National Trails System](/wiki/National_Trails_System "National Trails System") is a joint mission of the NPS, the [Bureau of Land Management](/wiki/Bureau_of_Land_Management "Bureau of Land Management") and the [US Forest Service](/wiki/United_States_Forest_Service "United States Forest Service"). It was created in 1968 to establish a system of long-distance [National Scenic](/wiki/National_Scenic_Trail "National Scenic Trail") and [National Historic Trails](/wiki/National_Historic_Trail "National Historic Trail"), as well as to recognize existing trails in the states as [National Recreation Trails](/wiki/National_Recreation_Trail "National Recreation Trail"). Several additional trails have been established since 1968, and in 2009 Congress established the first [National Geologic Trail](/wiki/Ice_Age_Floods_National_Geologic_Trail "Ice Age Floods National Geologic Trail").[[108]](#cite_note-110)

### National Heritage Areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: National Heritage Areas")]

[National Heritage Areas](/wiki/National_Heritage_Area "National Heritage Area") are a unique blend of natural, cultural, historic, and scenic resources. These are not considered units of the NPS, as they are maintained by state/territorial governments or non-profit organizations (described as *local coordinating entities*). The National Park Service provides an advisory role and limited technical, planning and financial assistance. Designation of National Heritage Areas is done by an Act of Congress. As of 2021 there are 55 designated heritage areas, some of which cross state lines.

## Initiatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Initiatives")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Preservewildlifeb.jpg/250px-Preservewildlifeb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Preservewildlifeb.jpg)

"The national parks preserve all life", poster for National Park Service, 1940

* 24-hr all Taxa BioBlitz: A joint venture of the [National Geographic Society](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society") and the NPS. Beginning in 2004, at [Rock Creek Parkway](/wiki/Rock_Creek_Parkway "Rock Creek Parkway"), the National Geographic Society and the NPS began a 10-year program of hosting a major biological survey of ten selected national park units. The intent is to develop public interest in the nation's natural resources, develop scientific interest in America's youth and to create citizen scientists.
  + 2007: [Rock Creek Park](/wiki/Rock_Creek_Park "Rock Creek Park"), Washington D.C. 661 species[[109]](#cite_note-NGS-111)
  + 2008: [Santa Monica Mountains National Recreation Area](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Mountains_National_Recreation_Area "Santa Monica Mountains National Recreation Area"), Los Angeles, California. 1,700 species and more pending.[[109]](#cite_note-NGS-111)
  + 2009: [Indiana Dunes National Lakeshore](/wiki/Indiana_Dunes_National_Lakeshore "Indiana Dunes National Lakeshore"), near Chicago in northern Indiana. 1,716 species and still counting.[[110]](#cite_note-112)
  + 2010: [Biscayne National Park](/wiki/Biscayne_National_Park "Biscayne National Park"), Miami, Florida. 810 species were identified during this 24-hr event. As classification continues, more species will be added to the list.[[111]](#cite_note-Biscayne_BioBlitz_page-113)
  + 2011: [Saguaro National Park](/wiki/Saguaro_National_Park "Saguaro National Park"), Tucson, Arizona.[[111]](#cite_note-Biscayne_BioBlitz_page-113) During the 24 hours, 859 different species were identified, of which more than 400 were previously unknown in the park.[[112]](#cite_note-Arrowhead19-3-114)
  + 2012: [Rocky Mountain National Park](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_National_Park "Rocky Mountain National Park"), in [Estes Park](/wiki/Estes_Park "Estes Park"), In August 2012 489 species were identified.[[113]](#cite_note-115)
  + 2013: [Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve](/wiki/Jean_Lafitte_National_Historical_Park_and_Preserve "Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve"), in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"). May 17–18, 2013 in the park's Barataria Preserve.[[114]](#cite_note-116)
  + 2014: [Golden Gate National Recreation Area](/wiki/Golden_Gate_National_Recreation_Area "Golden Gate National Recreation Area")[[115]](#cite_note-webpage-117)
  + 2015: [Hawai'i Volcanoes National Park](/wiki/Hawai%27i_Volcanoes_National_Park "Hawai'i Volcanoes National Park")[[115]](#cite_note-webpage-117)
  + 2016: [Whiskeytown National Recreation Area](/wiki/Whiskeytown_National_Recreation_Area "Whiskeytown National Recreation Area"), [Cabrillo National Monument](/wiki/Cabrillo_National_Monument "Cabrillo National Monument"), [Channel Islands National Park](/wiki/Channel_Islands_National_Park "Channel Islands National Park"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[115]](#cite_note-webpage-117)
  + 2017: [Virgin Islands National Park](/wiki/Virgin_Islands_National_Park "Virgin Islands National Park")[[115]](#cite_note-webpage-117)
* Biological Diversity: Biological Diversity is the vast variety of life as identified through species and genetics. This variety is decreasing as people spread across the globe, altering areas to better meet their needs.[[116]](#cite_note-118)
* Climate Change: *Warming of the climate system is unequivocal, as is now evident from observations of increases in global average air and ocean temperatures, widespread melting of snow and ice, and rising global sea levels.* (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2007).[[117]](#cite_note-119)
* South Florida Restoration Initiative: Rescuing an Ecosystem in Peril: In partnership with the State of [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), and the [Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/United_States_Army_Corps_of_Engineers "United States Army Corps of Engineers"), the NPS is restoring the physical and biological processes of the South Florida ecosystem. Historically, this ecosystem contained some of the most diverse habitats on earth.[[118]](#cite_note-120)
* Vanishing Treasures Initiative: Ruins Preservation in the American Southwest: The Vanishing Treasures Initiative began in FY 1998 to reduce threats to prehistoric and historic sites and structures in 44 parks of the Intermountain Region. In 2002, the program expanded to include three parks in the Pacific West Region. The goal is to reduce backlogged work and to bring sites and structures up to a condition where routine maintenance activities can preserve them.[[119]](#cite_note-121)
* Wetlands: Wetlands includes [marshes](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh"), [swamps](/wiki/Swamp "Swamp"), and [bogs](/wiki/Bog "Bog"). These areas and the plants and animals adapted to these conditions spread from the arctic to the equator. The shrinking wetlands provide habitat for fish and wildlife, help clean water and reduce the impact of storms and floods on the surrounding communities.[[120]](#cite_note-122)
* Wildland Fire: Fires have been a natural part of park eco-systems. Many plants and some animals require a cycle of fire or flooding to be successful and productive. With the advent of human intervention and public access to parks, there are safety concerns for the visiting public.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

### Green Park Plan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Green Park Plan")]

In September 2010, the NPS released its Climate Change Response Strategy, followed in April 2012 by the Green Parks Plan.[[122]](#cite_note-green-124)

#### Climate Friendly Parks Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Climate Friendly Parks Program")]

The Climate Friendly Parks Program is a subset of the Green Parks Plan.[[122]](#cite_note-green-124) It was created in collaboration between the NPS and the [US Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency").[[123]](#cite_note-CFPP-125) The program is meant to measure and reduce greenhouse gases to help slow the effects of climate change.

Parks in the CFP program create and implement plans to reduce greenhouse gases through reducing energy and water use. Facilities are designed and retrofitted using sustainable materials. Alternative transportation systems are developed to reduce dependency on fossil fuels.[[124]](#cite_note-CFPposter-126) Parks in the program offer public education programs about how the parks are already affected.

The CFP program provides climate-friendly solutions to the visiting public, like using clean energy, reducing waste, and making smart transportation choices.[[125]](#cite_note-127) The CFP program can provide technical assistance, tools and resources for the parks and their neighboring communities to protect the natural and cultural resources.[[126]](#cite_note-128)

The large, isolated parks typically generate their own electricity and heat and must do so without spoiling the values that the visitors have come to experience. Pollution is emitted by the vehicles used to transport visitors around the often-vast expanses of the parks. Many parks have converted vehicles to [electric hybrids](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicles "Hybrid electric vehicles"), and substitute [diesel/electric hybrid buses](/wiki/Diesel-electric "Diesel-electric") for private automobiles. In 2001 it was estimated that replacement with [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") would eliminate 25 TPY emissions entirely.[[127]](#cite_note-129)

In 2010, the NPS estimated that reducing bottled water could eliminate 6,000 tons of carbon emissions and 8 million kilowatt-hours of electricity every year. The NPS Concessions office voiced concerns about concessions impacts.[[128]](#cite_note-Norton-130)

By 2014, 23 parks had banned disposable water bottles.[[129]](#cite_note-131) In 2015, the [International Bottled Water Association](/wiki/International_Bottled_Water_Association "International Bottled Water Association") stated the NPS was "leaving sugary drinks as a primary alternative", even though the Park Service provides water stations to refill bottles, "encouraging visitors to hydrate for free". The Water Association made the national parks one of its top lobbying targets. In July 2015 Rep. [Keith Rothfus](/wiki/Keith_Rothfus "Keith Rothfus") added a "last-minute" amendment into Congress's appropriations bill, blocking the NPS from funding or enforcing the program.[[130]](#cite_note-132) The NPS discontinued its ban on disposable water bottles in August 2017.[[131]](#cite_note-133)

### Cashless

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Park_Service&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Cashless")]

In an effort to save on cash processing and hand handling fees, 22 national parks have gone [cashless](/wiki/Cashless "Cashless") as of 2023. In September 2023, US Senator [Cynthia Lummis](/wiki/Cynthia_Lummis "Cynthia Lummis") (R-WY) proposed the "[Protecting Access to Recreation with Cash Act](/wiki/Protecting_Access_to_Recreation_with_Cash_Act "Protecting Access to Recreation with Cash Act")" (PARC) which would require national parks to accept cash as a form of payment for entrance fee.[[132]](#cite_note-Lummis-134) In April 2024, several NPS visitors sued seeking to restore cash as a payment form noting how [cash](/wiki/Cash "Cash") is [legal tender](/wiki/Legal_tender "Legal tender") suitable "for all public charges" and that the "additional [processing fees](/wiki/Processing_fee "Processing fee") that will be borne by NPS and by visitors who ultimately fund the federal government through taxes, in addition to personal [surcharges](/wiki/Surcharge_(payment_systems) "Surcharge (payment systems)") and [bank fees](/wiki/Bank_fee "Bank fee") visitors may incur under NPS cashless policy."[[133]](#cite_note-135)[[134]](#cite_note-136)[[135]](#cite_note-137)
